data.title: EXCLUSIVE: Silver's Boom Has Left Wall Street Baffled — The Real Story Is A Structural Shockwave
data.description:
For decades, silver needed inflation or crisis to rally. That dependency may finally be ending. Silver's latest surge isn't being powered by CPI surprises or geopolitical fear. Instead, it's being pulled higher by structural demand from AI data centers, semiconductor manufacturing, and renewable energy — a shift that could permanently change how the metal trades.

  • Track silver-tracking SLV ETF here.

From Macro Trade To Industrial Input

In an exclusive interview with Benzinga, Ed Egilinsky of Direxion highlighted how silver's usage in semiconductors and data centers is becoming "transformative in nature."

These aren't speculative trends. They're capital-intensive, policy-supported investments with long time horizons.

Why Inflation Is No Longer Required

Unlike past cycles, silver, and the iShares Silver ETF (NYSE:SLV) as a proxy, have rallied even as market volatility stayed subdued. “This is evident over the last two years where the VIX has been mostly muted, yet gold and silver have rallied sharply,” said Egilinsky. That suggests demand is coming from physical consumption rather than financial hedging.

Solar energy alone remains a major silver sink, and AI infrastructure adds another layer of steady, non-discretionary demand. “The weakening of the U.S. Dollar in 2025 also provided some added fuel to the silver rally,” noted Egilinsky.

Supply Constraints Add Fuel

Downstream bottlenecks and export restrictions have limited silver's ability to respond quickly to demand spikes. When consumption rises, prices don't gradually adjust — they gap higher.

That dynamic makes the shiny metal more sensitive to structural demand shifts than markets may be pricing in.

A Re-Rating, Not A Trade

Silver is increasingly behaving less like gold's volatile cousin and more like a strategic industrial metal tied to the global electrification and compute buildout.

Investor takeaway: If AI and solar continue scaling, silver's cycle may be breaking — and long-term allocations may start replacing short-term trades.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: Nano-Cap Brand Engagement Network Stock Rallies As Africa AI Deal Fuels Investor Buzz
data.description:
Brand Engagement Network Inc. (NASDAQ:BNAI) shares are up on Wednesday with a session volume of 44.40 million compared to the average volume of 6.26 million.

The stock’s rise comes as the company has secured a $2.050 million AI licensing partnership in Africa, which may enhance its market position while broader markets experienced mixed performance.

Strategic AI Alliance with Valio Technologies

The partnership involves a strategic alliance with Valio Technologies (Pty) Ltd, establishing an exclusive AI licensing framework for government and commercial markets across Africa.

This includes a memorandum of understanding with Nelson Mandela University to implement a mental health support initiative, which is particularly timely given the rising mental health challenges among students in the region.

In addition to the financial contribution, the deal grants Brand Engagement Network a 25% common equity ownership in the newly formed entity and a 35% revenue share across various offerings.

Key deal terms also include an exclusive license for government and private-sector markets across Africa and a perpetual term with the right of first refusal on any sale.

This partnership leverages the company’s proprietary technologies to deliver localized AI solutions, addressing growing regulatory requirements in African markets.

BNAI Outperforms with Strong Technical Momentum

The stock is currently trading 163.8% above its 20-day simple moving average (SMA) and 146.9% above its 100-day SMA, demonstrating strong short-term momentum. Shares have increased 2.81% over the past 12 months and are currently positioned closer to their 52-week highs than lows.

The RSI is at 68.76, which is considered neutral territory, suggesting that the stock is not yet overbought. Meanwhile, MACD is above its signal line, indicating bullish momentum.

The combination of neutral RSI and bullish MACD suggests mixed momentum.

  • Key Resistance: $10.50
  • Key Support: N/A

The stock carries a Buy Rating with an average price target of $6.00.

BNAI Scores High on Benzinga Edge Momentum

Below is the Benzinga Edge scorecard for Brand Engagement Network, highlighting its strengths and weaknesses compared to the broader market:

  • Momentum: Bullish (Score: 89.44/100) — Stock is outperforming the broader market.

The Verdict: Brand Engagement Network’s Benzinga Edge signal reveals a classic ‘High-Flyer’ setup. The strong momentum score confirms the stock’s upward trend, suggesting that investors may want to capitalize on this momentum while being cautious of potential volatility.

BNAI Price Action: Brand Engagement Network shares were up 57.75% at $9.70 on Wednesday, according to Benzinga Pro data.

Image via Shutterstock

data.title: Why Is Venus Concept Stock Plunging Today?
data.description:
Venus Concept Inc. (NASDAQ:VERO) shares fell on Wednesday after the company said it will voluntarily delist from Nasdaq and end SEC reporting.

The board notified Nasdaq that it plans to leave the Nasdaq Capital Market and deregister with the SEC, citing compliance costs that outweigh the benefits of staying listed.

As of September 30, 2025, the company's inventories totaled $15.768 million. Cash and equivalents totaled $4.312 million.

Where And When It Happens

Venus Concept said it plans to file a Form 25 with the SEC on or about January 30, 2026.

The company expects its last day of trading on Nasdaq to be on or about February 6, 2026.

The company also plans to file a Form 15 on or about February 9, 2026. That filing will immediately suspend its obligation to submit periodic SEC reports, including Forms 10-K, 10-Q and 8-K.

The board said it reviewed the cost of ongoing SEC reporting and determined it exceeds the value of remaining publicly listed.

"The Board of Directors, after thorough evaluation, has determined this action is in the best interests of the Company and its shareholders," said Rajiv De Silva, chief executive officer of Venus Concept.

He said the company will continue working with Madryn Asset Management to support long-term financial success.

Venus Concept sells medical aesthetic technology, including minimally invasive and non-invasive devices, in more than 60 countries.

Its portfolio includes Venus NOVA, Venus Versa and the ARTAS iX robotic hair restoration system.

VERO Price Action: Venus Concept shares were down 54.83% at $2.00 at the time of publication on Wednesday. The stock is near its 52-week low of $1.39, according to Benzinga Pro data.

Image via Shutterstock

data.title: Kraft Heinz Stock Sinks As Berkshire Heads For The Exits
data.description:
The Kraft Heinz Co (NASDAQ:KHC) shares are under pressure Wednesday after Berkshire Hathaway Inc (NYSE:BRK) (NYSE:BRK) filed to potentially sell its stake in the company. Here’s what you need to know.

  • Kraft Heinz stock is at significant support. What’s behind KHC weakness?

Berkshire’s Intent: A Major Shift Ahead?

On Jan. 20, Kraft Heinz submitted a prospectus supplement to the SEC registering the potential resale of up to 325,442,152 shares held by Berkshire. The move comes from a 2015 Amended and Restated Registration Rights Agreement between Kraft Heinz, 3G Global Food Holdings and Berkshire Hathaway.

Berkshire now has the ability to sell its stake in the company if it chooses. Kraft Heinz would not receive any proceeds from such sales, and the company itself is not issuing new stock as part of the deal.

The possibility of Berkshire selling up to 325 million shares is weighing on investor sentiment, as a sale of that caliber could create meaningful selling pressure in the stock.

Navigating Kraft Heinz's Bearish Market Landscape

Kraft Heinz is currently positioned in a bearish setup, trading significantly below its key moving averages. Kraft Heinz is trading 6.3% below its 20-day SMA, 8.3% below its 50-day SMA, and 15.2% below its 200-day SMA, indicating a lack of upward momentum.

The RSI is currently at 46.56, which is considered neutral territory. This suggests that there isn’t strong buying or selling pressure at the moment, leaving traders in a wait-and-see mode regarding potential price action.

MACD is above its signal line, indicating some bullish momentum, but it's important to note that this could be short-lived given the overall bearish trend. Traders should watch for any shifts in momentum that might suggest a stronger move in either direction.

Key resistance is set at $26.00, and if Kraft Heinz’s stock approaches this level, it could signal a potential reversal or continuation of the current downtrend if it fails to break through. Conversely, without a clear support level identified, any further declines could lead to increased selling pressure.

Kraft Heinz has experienced a challenging 12-month period, down 24.07%. This longer-term trend reflects ongoing struggles and suggests that traders should remain cautious, as the stock is currently hitting new 52-week lows.

KHC Price Action: Kraft Heinz shares were down 5.81% at $22.39 at the time of publication on Wednesday. The stock is trading at a new 52-week low, according to Benzinga Pro.

Image: Jonathan Weiss/Shutterstock

data.title: 10 Tax Laws Small Businesses In The US Should Know
data.description:
Running a small business in the United States means operating inside a tax system that is highly structured, rules-based, and often unforgiving. Taxes don't just determine how much you owe in April. They affect cash flow, hiring decisions, investment timing, and how resilient a business can be during slow periods.

Below are ten U.S. tax laws and rules that consistently shape how small businesses are taxed, based on IRS guidance and federal policy that applies to most owners.

1. The Pay-As-You-Go Tax System

The U.S. tax system operates on a pay-as-you-go basis. That means taxes are expected to be paid throughout the year as income is earned, not settled in one lump sum at filing time. The IRS explains that business owners who expect to owe tax must generally make estimated payments under its rules for estimated taxes.

Missing these payments can trigger penalties and interest, even if the full tax bill is eventually paid.

2. Estimated Quarterly Tax Deadlines

Most sole proprietors, freelancers, and pass-through business owners must estimate income and pay taxes quarterly. These payments usually cover both income tax and self-employment tax.

The IRS outlines who must pay and how to calculate these amounts in its self-employed tax center, emphasizing that underpayment penalties apply regardless of intent.

3. Cash Versus Accrual Accounting Rules

Small businesses may be eligible to choose between cash and accrual accounting, but the choice affects when income and expenses are taxed. Under the cash method, income is reported when received. Under accrual, income is reported when earned.

The IRS explains eligibility and limitations in its guidance on accounting methods. As businesses grow, revenue thresholds can restrict which method is allowed.

4. Inventory Treatment And Deduction Rules

Businesses that sell physical goods must follow specific inventory rules. Inventory costs generally cannot be deducted immediately and must be accounted for separately from other expenses.

The IRS explains how inventory must be handled and when costs become deductible in its overview of inventory accounting. Misclassifying inventory is a common source of reporting errors.

5. The Home Office Deduction

Owners who regularly and exclusively use part of their home for business may qualify for a home office deduction. The deduction is based on the percentage of the home used for business and can include utilities, insurance, and depreciation.

The IRS sets strict standards for eligibility and calculation in its guidance on the home office deduction, making documentation essential.

6. Meals And Entertainment Deduction Limits

Entertainment expenses are no longer deductible, a change that followed the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act. Business meals, however, may still be partially deductible if specific conditions are met.

The IRS explains what qualifies and what does not in its guidance on business-related meals, including record-keeping requirements.

7. Qualified Business Income Deduction (Section 199A)

Certain pass-through business owners may qualify for a deduction of up to 20% of qualified business income. Eligibility depends on income levels, business type, and whether the business is classified as a specified service trade.

The IRS details how the qualified business income deduction works, including phaseouts and limitations that apply as income rises.

8. Bonus Depreciation Rules

Bonus depreciation allows businesses to deduct a significant portion of qualifying asset costs in the year the asset is placed into service rather than spreading deductions over time.

The IRS explains current rules and scheduled phase-downs in its overview of bonus depreciation. These rules can materially affect equipment and expansion decisions.

9. Section 179 Expensing

Section 179 allows qualifying businesses to expense the full cost of certain equipment and property in the year it is purchased and placed in service, subject to annual limits.

The IRS provides detailed eligibility rules and thresholds in its guidance on the Section 179 deduction, making it a common planning tool for profitable years.

10. Retirement Contribution Timing For Business Owners

Business owners can often deduct retirement plan contributions made before filing their tax return, even if those contributions apply to the prior tax year.

The IRS explains contribution deadlines and plan types in its overview of retirement plan contribution deadlines, giving owners flexibility in managing both taxes and cash flow.

Why These Rules Matter Together

Each of these laws governs a specific part of the tax code. Together, they determine when income is taxed, how expenses are recognized, and how much flexibility a business has in managing cash flow year to year.

The U.S. Small Business Administration stresses that understanding federal tax responsibilities is critical to long-term viability, noting that tax compliance failures are a common source of financial strain, as outlined in its guide to paying federal business taxes.

Final Perspective

These tax laws are not loopholes or optional strategies. They form the operating framework for small businesses in the U.S. Understanding them does not eliminate tax obligations, but it reduces uncertainty, improves planning, and lowers the risk of costly mistakes.

For many small business owners, mastering these rules is not about paying less tax. It is about staying predictable, compliant, and financially stable as the business grows.

Benzinga Disclaimer: This article is from an unpaid external contributor. It does not represent Benzinga’s reporting and has not been edited for content or accuracy.
